metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: Destructive/irreversible operation(s) with no stated safeguard.
findings.suggested_fix: State the safeguard (backup, dry-run, reversible migration, rollback) or justify the irreversibility explicitly (T4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact extraction rule for the leading `<id>:` subject token (e.g., 'first whitespace-delimited token on line 1 that ends with `:`, after stripping the colon, AND passes the safety regex') and clarify whether conventional-commit prefixes like `fix:` or `feat:` should be excluded (e.g., by requiring the token to pass `resolve_ticket_id`'s shape check before being considered a candidate).
findings.finding: The extraction rule for the 'leading <id>: subject token' form is under-specified: the plan says extract it but does not state the precise parse (e.g., 'first whitespace-delimited token on line 1 that matches /^[^:]+:$/, strip the colon' vs. 'first word before any colon anywhere on the subject line'). An implementing agent must guess, and different guesses yield different behavior for subjects like 'fix: do thing' (conventional-commit style) vs. 'ABC-123: do thing'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — verify_commit.py / extract_ticket_refs bullet; also CLI bullet ('valid leading-<id>: commit → exit 0')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Leading subject token: match `^([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9._-]{0,127}):` at the very start of line 1 (before any space); the captured group is the candidate. Conventional-commit keywords (fix, feat, chore, …) will fail resolve_ticket_id and be reported as tried-but-unresolved, which is intentional — contributors must use a ticket ID, not a CC prefix, as the leading token.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test cases to `tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py` for each I/O error path the CLI AC promises: (a) bad `--rev` produces a git-failure error (not a missing-ticket diagnostic), (b) missing `--message-file` names the file, and (c) store not found produces a store-error message — asserting that each exit is non-zero AND that the output text is distinguishable from `EXPECTED_FORMAT`.
findings.finding: The test AC for `tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py` does not enumerate tests for the three I/O error cases that the CLI AC explicitly promises will produce DISTINGUISHABLE non-zero exits: (a) bad `--rev` / git failure, (b) missing `--message-file`, and (c) store not found / unreadable. These are new user-facing failure flows; without pinned tests the distinguishability invariant can silently regress.
findings.location: Tests AC — `tests/unit/test_verify_commit_ticket.py` bullet; CLI AC — I/O robustness paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add three parametrized or separate test functions in the test file, e.g. `test_bad_rev_is_distinguishable_from_missing_ticket`, `test_missing_message_file_error`, `test_store_not_found_error`, each asserting `returncode != 0` and that the diagnostic does NOT contain the `EXPECTED_FORMAT` trailer-format text (proving distinguishability).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 'Store resolution' AC bullet: `verify_commit_message` only needs `config.tracker_dir(root)` — `config.tickets_branch()` is used by the CI worktree-fetch step in the YAML, not by the Python resolver; remove it from the Python function's dependency list.
findings.finding: The AC's 'Store resolution' bullet asserts `verify_commit_message` uses both `config.tracker_dir()` AND `config.tickets_branch()`, but `resolve_ticket_id(ticket_id, tracker_dir)` only accepts a filesystem path — it never consults a git branch. `tickets_branch()` is used by git push/sync/reconciler code, not by the resolver. Listing it as a dependency of `verify_commit_message` is incorrect and may cause the implementer to add a spurious `tickets_branch()` call inside the Python function.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Store resolution uses the standard config helpers `config.tracker_dir()` / `config.tickets_branch()`' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC bullet to: 'Store resolution uses `config.tracker_dir(root)` (overridable via `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` / `tracker.dir`). `config.tickets_branch()` is only referenced by the CI step that fetches the branch into a worktree — it is not a parameter of `verify_commit_message`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to the `extract_ticket_refs` spec (or a `# Decisions` bullet) acknowledging `git interpret-trailers --parse` and explaining why a custom parser is used instead (e.g. 'the security-sanitization regex must run before any store lookup, and `git interpret-trailers` is not available inside the pure-Python test harness / adds a subprocess dependency for what is otherwise in-process logic').
findings.finding: The plan's `extract_ticket_refs` introduces bespoke trailer parsing (case-insensitive `rebar-ticket:` header scan, whitespace split, `()`,`,` stripping) without acknowledging `git interpret-trailers --parse`, which is the native git built-in for exactly this job and has been stable since git 2.6 (2015). The plan does not consider this prior art or document why a custom parser is preferred.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'operator throwaway change' note with a concrete verification procedure: name the steps (e.g., push a Gerrit change to main with no `rebar-ticket:` trailer, confirm `require-ticket` job exits 1 and Verified is set to -1; then push one with a valid trailer and confirm Verified +1), and state the observable success signal.
findings.finding: The CI end-to-end verification is stated as 'proved by an operator throwaway change, like the two-vote E2E' — a reference to an external procedure with no concrete steps, no named actor, and no success criterion, which makes it unverifiable from the plan alone.
findings.location: Verification section — 'CI-path (require-ticket job) is proved by an operator throwaway change, like the two-vote E2E.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note (e.g., in the CI AC or a new 'Rollback' subsection) stating that setting `verify.require_ticket_for_commit = false` in `rebar.toml` disables the gate without touching `gerrit-verify.yaml`, and that this is the intended fast-disable path.
findings.finding: The plan changes the `gerrit-verify.yaml` dependency graph (adding `require-ticket` to `needs` of `build-and-test` and `vote`) — a behavioral change to existing CI — but never explicitly names a rollback/back-out path for the case where the new job proves persistently flaky or misconfigured in production. The config flag `verify.require_ticket_for_commit` implicitly serves as a disable switch (exit 0 when false), but the plan neither frames it as the rollback mechanism nor states 'to disable enforcement, set the flag to false in rebar.toml and re-push'.
findings.location: CI AC / gerrit-verify.yaml section; Config AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CI AC or a 'Rollback' subsection: 'To disable enforcement rapidly (e.g., persistent infra failure), set `verify.require_ticket_for_commit = false` in `rebar.toml` and push; the `require-ticket` job will exit 0, unblocking `build-and-test` and `vote` without YAML changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare the authentication contract for the `git fetch https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} tickets` step in the CI spec: either assert that `checkout-gerrit-change-action` pre-configures git credentials for the same repo (and name the mechanism), or add `token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}` / a `git remote set-url` with embedded token step so the fetch is unambiguously authenticated for both public and private repo scenarios.
findings.finding: The CI fetch step `git fetch https://github.com/${{ github.repository }} '+tickets:refs/remotes/gh-tickets'` does not declare whether it relies on pre-configured git credentials (from `checkout-gerrit-change-action`) or requires an explicit token. For a private repository, an unauthenticated HTTPS fetch will fail at best and may expose credential-handling ambiguity; the plan is silent on the authentication contract for this boundary-crossing network call.
findings.location: CI section — `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`, 'mount the authoritative tickets store' step
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a step before the fetch that sets the authenticated remote URL explicitly:
```yaml
- name: Configure git credentials for tickets fetch
  run: |
    git remote add gh-origin "https://x-access-token:${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}@github.com/${{ github.repository }}" 2>/dev/null || true
```
Then replace the bare `https://github.com/...` in the fetch with `gh-origin`, or use the token-embedded URL directly in the fetch. Document in the plan that `GITHUB_TOKEN` is the credential used.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
